meta.title: Mercedes-Benz OM651 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz OM651 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes OM651 is Mercedes' most common modern diesel four — dependable, but the 'Black Death' injector seal issue is well documented. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

rows.value: Confidence: 92% confidence — based on 1,250 UK enquiries and cross-referenced specialist rebuild reports.

rows.metric: Mix of cheap fixes (injector seals) and labour-intensive work (timing chain)

rows.value: Parts cheap and available from multiple UK suppliers; high-volume engine

rows.metric: Recon cost as % of lowest host value (~£4k) = 95% → over 75% = 4

rows.value: Sum of the five rows above

rows.metric: One-line verdict: The OM651 is a strong, economical diesel four — the 'Black Death' injector seal issue is well-documented but preventable — insist on full service history.
rows.value: Best for: High-mileage drivers who service on schedule.

rows.metric: Avoid if: No documented injector seal or chain history above 80,000 miles.

The OM651 is Mercedes‑Benz’s most prolific modern four‑cylinder diesel, powering millions of cars and vans across the globe. Introduced in 2008, it replaced the OM646 and was offered in numerous power levels, from 109 hp in commercial applications to 204 hp in passenger cars.
* Excellent fuel economy – often exceeding 50 mpg in real‑world driving
rows.value: * Strong low‑end torque – makes for relaxed cruising

rows.metric: * Proven durability – many examples exceed 200,000 miles with routine maintenance
rows.value: * Wide parts availability – both genuine and aftermarket support is exceptional
* Injector seal failure (“Black Death”) – carbon buildup around injectors, causing rough running and potential damage if ignored. Early detection is key.

rows.metric: * Timing chain tensioner wear – can lead to chain stretch and rattling on cold start (TSB LI80.20‑P‑050001).
rows.value: * DPF and EGR clogging – particularly in urban‑use vehicles; requires occasional regeneration or cleaning.

rows.metric: * Oil cooler leaks – less common than on OM642 but still reported.

rows.value: The OM651 is a dependable workhorse when maintained correctly. Regular oil changes, injector seal inspections, and occasional DPF‑friendly driving will ensure a long life.

rows.variantBadge: C200 CDI, C220 CDI, C250 CDI
E200 CDI, E220 CDI, E250 CDI

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an OM651 typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE].

cta.label: → Compare OM651 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: How much does an OM651 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned OM651 typically costs £2,800–£4,800 supply only [MB-QUOTE], varying by power variant and whether ancillaries are included. Fitting adds £520–£1,100 depending on labour rates and vehicle model.

items.question: Is the OM651 a reliable engine?
items.answer: The OM651 rates 4.0★ out of 5 based on 1,250 UK enquiries and specialist reports [MB-VERIFIED]. It is generally robust, but injector seal and timing chain issues are well known and require proactive maintenance.

items.question: What is the most common OM651 failure?
items.answer: Injector seal failure (the “Black Death” – carbon buildup around the injector) is the most frequently reported issue, typically from 80,000–150,000 miles. Timing chain tensioner wear also occurs, causing cold-start rattle.

items.question: Which Mercedes models use the OM651?
items.answer: The OM651 powers C‑Class, E‑Class, GLC, GLA, GLK, Sprinter, Vito, V‑Class, and some S‑Class and ML‑Class variants – covering cars and vans from 2008 to 2020.

items.question: How long does the OM651 last?
items.answer: With regular oil changes and timely injector seal replacements, the OM651 routinely exceeds 200,000 miles. Many specialist examples are reported at 250,000+ with no major internal work [MB-INTERVIEW].
